mappings.rationaleAI: The late Tang period (755-907) including the An Lushan rebellion and its effects (A.1.a), growing Buddhist influences and cultural developments (A.1.b), and social and economic developments such as decline of aristocracy and agricultural advances (A.1.c).
Mongol-era facilitation of the Silk Road relates to the expansion of trade under Yuan/Mongol rule (B.1.b), and commercial and cultural contacts between Yuan China and the West (B.2.b).

The Mongol Empire's conquest of China under Genghis Khan and Kublai Khan, establishment of the Yuan dynasty, and administrative reforms directly address Mongol-Chinese rule (B), including the conquest (B.1.a) and government and administration with capital at Ta-tu/Peking (B.1.b).
The post-classical period's coverage of Tang and Song dynasty China, neo-Confucianism, gunpowder, and the Mongol Empire corresponds to the Sung dynasty era (A) and Yuan dynasty Mongol rule (B).
Comprehensive coverage of Tang, Song, Ming, and Qing dynastic rule provides broad context for the late T'ang and Sung (A), Yuan dynasty (B), and Ming and Ch'ing dynasties (C).
Genghis Khan's conquest of the Chin (Jin) dynasty in northern China directly addresses the Mongol conquest of China and the beginning of Mongol rule (B.1.a).
Confucius's teachings evolving into Neo-Confucianism during Tang and Song dynasties directly relates to the Sung cultural development of resurgent Neo-Confucianism (A.3).
The Islamic Golden Age's commercial and cultural exchanges are tangentially relevant to Yuan China's relations with the West and commercial contacts (B.2.b).
Beijing as Ta-tu under Kublai Khan's Yuan dynasty capital (B.1.b) and as Peking under Ming and Ch'ing dynastic governance (C.1.a) is directly relevant.
Coverage of the Mongol Empire, medieval Chinese dynasties, and Qing decline provides context across the Sung era (A), Yuan period (B), and Ming/Ch'ing dynasties (C).
The Black Death's transmission via Mongol trade networks from Central Asia is relevant to religious and intellectual life and relations with the West during Yuan China (B.2).
East Asian regional history with Chinese civilisation's cultural dominance provides context for the Sung cultural developments (A) and Ming/Ch'ing tributary system (C).

Trade history from ancient networks through medieval commercial systems relates to Sung economic developments and expansion of trade (A.1.c), Mongol expansion of trade (B.1.b), and Ming foreign relations and economic policy (C.1.b).
Agricultural history in China is relevant to Sung agricultural advances (A.1.c) and mid-Ch'ing advances through increased rice cultivation and introduction of new crops (C.2.b.i).
Agricultural production systems and Neolithic domestication through later developments are relevant to Sung agricultural advances (A.1.c) and Ch'ing agricultural expansion (C.2.b.i).
